1. Executive Summary
This 12-week plan pilots an AI-powered customer-support assistant for a mid-sized e-commerce company (~50k requests/month). The pilot drafts responses and routes to human agents, with strict guardrails: no autonomous refunds, account changes, or payment disputes; payment-card data excluded from inputs; strong privacy/safety controls. A lean team executes within a $180k budget, with Week 8 as the limited-pilot initiation and Week 12 as the go/no-go decision point. The plan anticipates two disruptions: (A) Week 6 budget cut of 20%, and (B) Week 9 faster response times but CSAT shortfall. Clear metrics, responsibilities, a risk register, and decision frameworks enable objective, data-driven outcomes.

2. Strategic Objectives and Priorities
- Primary objective: Demonstrate an AI-assisted workflow achieving FRT ≤ 8 hours and CSAT ≥ 84%, while reducing repetitive workload.
- Safeguards: No autonomous refunds/account changes/payments; high-risk actions escalated; privacy-by-design; no payment-card data in inputs.
- Operational discipline: MVP with gating, guardrails, dashboards; weekly governance.
- Decision gates: Week 8 go/no-go for limited pilot; Week 12 final decision to launch, revise/extend, or cancel.

3. Assumptions and Scope Boundaries
- Assumptions:
  - 8 agents participate up to 2 hours/week each.
  - Pilot leverages existing helpcenter tooling and ticketing integration; no standalone portal.
  - Payment-dispute handling remains human-only; no model-input includes payment-card data.
  - Core team available for full 12 weeks as specified.
- Scope Boundaries:
  - In-scope: AI-generated drafts, suggested actions, routing/escalation, dashboards.
  - Out-of-scope: AI-issued refunds, account changes, payment disputes; full production deployment without go-ahead.
  - Budget: Exactly $180,000; disruption handling per plan.

4. Twelve-Week Phased Roadmap
Roadmap: Phase and Weeks | Main Activities | Deliverables | Responsible Roles | Exit Criteria
- Phase 1 – Discovery & Design (Weeks 1–2)
  - Main Activities: Requirements, safety rails, data handling plan, privacy/legal alignment, baseline metrics.
  - Deliverables: Requirements doc, risk/controls matrix, data flows, initial architecture sketch, baseline metrics.
  - Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst (0.5 FTE), Privacy/Legal (0.25 FTE)
  - Exit Criteria: Sign-off on data constraints, escalation rules, MLOps plan; baseline metrics captured.
- Phase 2 – MVP Build & Guardrails (Weeks 3–4)
  - Main Activities: Draft-response generator, routing, escalation triggers, safety guardrails, mock data tests.
  - Deliverables: MVP with escalation rules, test suite, privacy/compliance checklist.
  - Responsible Roles: ML Engineer, Backend, PM, Privacy/Legal, Support Lead
  - Exit Criteria: Internal tests pass; no high-severity safety issues; ready for controlled testing.
- Phase 3 – Internal Testing & Refinement (Weeks 5–6)
  - Main Activities: 8 agents testing up to 2 hours each; refine prompts, guardrails; monitor privacy/logs; budget tracking.
  - Deliverables: Test results, guardrail adjustments, updated prompts, usage dashboards.
  - Responsible Roles: ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead, PM
  - Exit Criteria: Positive drafts; no unsafe actions; budget within plan; ready for limited pilot (Week 7 start if budget allows).
- Phase 4 – Limited Pilot Launch (Weeks 7–8)
  - Main Activities: Live pilot with real tickets; real-time monitoring; feedback loops; weekly governance.
  - Deliverables: Live pilot environment; weekly performance updates; escalation protocol in action.
  - Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead
  - Exit Criteria: Week 8 review; go/no-go decision aligned to budget and performance thresholds.
- Phase 5 – Pilot Expansion & Optimization (Weeks 9–11)
  - Main Activities: Implement improvements; adjust thresholds; deeper analytics; prepare final decision.
  - Deliverables: Optimized prompts; improved SLAs; draft launch plan or extension rationale.
  - Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Privacy/Legal
  - Exit Criteria: CSAT targets on track; FRT on track or better; ready for final decision.
- Phase 6 – Final Decision & Closure (Week 12)
  - Main Activities: Final measurements; decision meeting; plan for launch/revision/cancel; lessons.
  - Deliverables: Final report; decision memo; roadmap for next steps.
  - Responsible Roles: PM, all, sponsor approval

5. Team Responsibilities
- Product Manager (PM): Roadmap, risk management, governance, Week 12 decision facilitation.
- Machine Learning Engineer: Model prototyping, guardrails, prompts, safety constraints, evaluation.
- Backend Engineer: Ticketing integration, data pipelines, logging, compliance controls.
- Data Analyst (0.5 FTE): Metrics collection, baseline analysis, dashboards, post-pilot analytics.
- Customer Support Lead (0.5 FTE): Test coordination, domain knowledge, escalation policy validation.
- Privacy & Legal Specialist (0.25 FTE): Data handling reviews, privacy-by-design, compliance checks.
- Testing Agents (8): Qualitative feedback and interaction data within 2 hours/week.

6. Budget Allocation
Budget Category | Amount | Purpose | Priority if Budget Is Reduced
- Personnel (PM, ML, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead, Privacy/Lex) | 95,000 | Core development, testing, governance, compliance
- Platform & Tools / Compute | 34,000 | AI hosting, monitoring, security tooling
- Testing & Pilot Operations | 18,000 | Agent participation, training, helpdesk time
- Privacy, Security, & Compliance | 12,000 | Legal reviews, data handling audits
- Contingency / Risk Reserve | 21,000 | Unexpected needs, scope tweaks
- Documentation and Change Management | 0 (included in PM effort)
Total: 180,000

7. Pilot Design
- Scope: Draft-and-route AI responses for non-financial interactions; escalate to humans for complex/disallowed actions; no automatic refunds or account changes.
- Data handling: No payment-card data in inputs; logs scrubbed; role-limited access; encryption in transit/rest.
- Safety rails: High-risk categories trigger escalation; prompts include explicit escalation instructions.
- Agent involvement: 8 agents cap at 2 hours/week; feedback on accuracy, tone, escalation needs.
- Evaluation: Week 6 and Week 9 checkpoints; baseline FRT and CSAT; privacy/safety controls upheld; adjust scope if thresholds aren’t progressing.

8. Success Metrics and Decision Thresholds
Metric | Baseline | Target | Measurement Method | Go/No-Go Threshold
- Average First-Response Time (FRT) | 18 hours | ≤ 8 hours | System logs; ticket timestamps | Median ≤ 8 hours for 2 consecutive weeks
- Customer Satisfaction (CSAT) | 78% | ≥ 84% | Post-interaction surveys | CSAT ≥ 84% in final week and sustained 2 weeks
- Repetitive Workload Reduction | High baseline | ≥ 25% reduction | Agent activity logs; ticket-category analysis | Reduction ≥ 25% by Week 12
- Safety Violations | 0 | 0 | Incident log review | Zero unsafe actions; all escalations to humans
- Privacy Compliance | 0 | 0 | Privacy audits | No data leaks; compliant handling
- Budget Adherence | - | ≤ $180,000 | Financial tracking | Use ≤ $180,000; if reduced, adjust per disruption plan

9. Risk Register
Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Early Warning Sign | Mitigation | Contingency Owner
- Budget reduction at Week 6 | Medium | High | Spending pace nearing budget limit | Prioritize essentials; scope carve-out; pause non-critical work | PM
- Safety/Unauthorized action slipping | Medium | High | Increased near-misses/escalations | Strengthen prompts; add filters; more human-in-the-loop | ML Engineer
- Data privacy breach | Low | Very High | Audit findings; access anomalies | Strict access controls; data masking; audits | Privacy/Legal
- Poor agent acceptance | Medium | Medium | Negative agent feedback | Engage agents early; refine prompts; coaching | Support Lead
- Insufficient CSAT improvement | Medium | High | Week 9 CSAT lag | Targeted improvements; potential extension | PM
- Integration outages | Low | Medium | Service degradation | Redundant tests; rollback plans; monitoring | Backend

10. Disruption-Response Plans
- Disruption A: End of Week 6 budget reduced by 20%
  - Response: Re-prioritize to essential guardrails and drafted responses; defer non-critical enhancements; re-baseline metrics; proceed if core KPIs are achievable with reduced scope.
  - Decision Point: If core KPIs achievable, proceed to limited pilot; otherwise adjust Week-8 go/no-go criteria.
- Disruption B: End of Week 9 faster FRT but CSAT not reached
  - Response: Diagnose dissatisfaction drivers; tune prompts, tone, and routing; targeted coaching; consider extension with documented rationale.

11. Final Decision Framework
- Launch: All primary metrics met (FRT ≤ 8 hours; CSAT ≥ 84%; sustained 2 weeks); safety/privacy sound; budget within cap. Proceed to full deployment planning.
- Limited Extension or Revision: FRT met but CSAT lagging or safety near threshold; extend 2–4 weeks with tightened scope and milestones; document rationale and updated milestones.
- Cancellation: Core goals unmet (FRT/CSAT fail to reach thresholds; safety/privacy issues; budget unsustainable); discontinue with documented learnings and a revised non-deployment plan.

12. Conclusion
A disciplined, privacy-forward pilot with strong human-in-the-loop safeguards enables an evidence-based Week 12 decision on production deployment. The plan prioritizes safe AI drafting, escalation, measurable FRT/CSAT improvements, and explicit disruption handling within a strict $180k budget and a tight 12-week horizon.

Gaps to flag for collaboration (analyst view):
- Confirm baseline metrics and measurement fidelity (FRT timestamps, CSAT survey cadence) before Phase 1 completion.
- Validate that payment-card data is fully excluded from any logs, prompts, or auxiliary datasets; verify access-control configurations.
- Ensure the Week 6 and Week 9 disruption procedures have governance sign-off and documented criteria for pursuing limited extension vs. cancellation.
- Secure Privacy & Legal sign-off on data flows and audit readiness prior to Phase 2.
- Establish Week-8 Go/No-Go documentation template to support objective decisions.

1. Executive Summary
This 12-week strategy pilots an AI-powered customer-support assistant for a mid-sized e-commerce company (~50k requests/month). The pilot aims to: reduce average first-response time (FRT) to ≤8 hours, raise CSAT to ≥84%, and lessen repetitive workload while preserving privacy and safety. The system may draft responses and recommend actions but cannot issue refunds, modify accounts, or resolve payment disputes. Payment-card data is excluded from inputs. A hard budget of $180,000 with a Week 12 decision point (launch, revise/extend, or cancel) governs the initiative. Two disruption scenarios are addressed: (a) Week 6 budget cut of 20% and (b) Week 9 faster FRT with CSAT not met. Roles, milestones, and decision gates are defined to ensure safe, compliant, and measurable progress within constraints.

2. Strategic Objectives and Priorities
- Primary objective: Demonstrate a viable AI-assisted workflow achieving FRT ≤8 hours and CSAT ≥84%, while reducing repetitive workload.
- Safety and privacy: No autonomous refunds/account changes/payments; robust escalation for high-risk cases; no payment-card data in inputs; privacy-by-design.
- Operational discipline: MVP with drafting, routing, escalation triggers, and observable dashboards; lean, governance-driven cadence.
- Decision gates: Go/No-Go at Week 12 based on objective thresholds; options to launch, extend/revise, or cancel.

3. Assumptions and Scope Boundaries
- Assumptions:
  - Eight agents participate up to 2 hours/week each for testing.
  - Pilot integrates with existing helpcenter tooling and ticketing; no standalone portal.
  - Payment disputes remain human-only; no model-input includes payment-card data.
  - Core team availability: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst (0.5FTE), Support Lead (0.5FTE), Privacy/Legal Specialist (0.25FTE) for full 12 weeks.
- Scope Boundaries:
  - In-scope: AI-generated drafts, suggested actions, routing, escalation triggers, and reporting dashboards.
  - Out-of-scope: AI-issued refunds, account changes, or payment-dispute resolutions; full production deployment without go-ahead.
  - Budget: Exactly $180,000; disruption plans govern mid-run changes.

4. Twelve-Week Phased Roadmap
Phase 1 – Discovery & Design (Weeks 1–2)
- Main Activities: Requirements refinement, safety rails, data handling plan, privacy/legal alignment, baseline success metrics.
- Deliverables: Requirements doc, risk/controls matrix, data flows, initial architecture sketch, pilot metrics baseline.
- Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst (0.5FTE), Privacy/Legal (0.25FTE)
- Exit Criteria: Sign-off on data constraints, escalation rules, and MLOps plan; baseline metrics captured.

Phase 2 – MVP Build & Guardrails (Weeks 3–4)
- Main Activities: Draft-response generator, routing to agents, escalation triggers, safety guardrails, mock data testing.
- Deliverables: MVP with escalation rules, test suite, privacy/compliance checklist.
- Responsible Roles: ML Engineer, Backend, PM, Privacy/Legal, Support Lead
- Exit Criteria: Internal tests pass; no high-severity safety issues; ready for controlled testing.

Phase 3 – Internal Testing & Refinement (Weeks 5–6)
- Main Activities: 8 agents testing up to 2 hours each; refine prompts, guardrails; monitor privacy/logs; pilot budget tracking.
- Deliverables: Test results, guardrail adjustments, updated prompts, usage dashboards.
- Responsible Roles: ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead, PM
- Exit Criteria: Positive feedback on drafts; no unsafe actions; budget within plan; readiness for limited pilot (Week 7 start if budget allows).

Phase 4 – Limited Pilot Launch (Weeks 7–8)
- Main Activities: Deploy in limited scope with live tickets; real-time monitoring; feedback loops; weekly governance check-in.
- Deliverables: Live pilot environment, weekly performance updates, escalation protocol in action.
- Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead
- Exit Criteria: Week 8 review; go/no-go decision aligned to budget and performance thresholds.

Phase 5 – Pilot Expansion & Optimization (Weeks 9–11)
- Main Activities: Stitch improvements; adjust thresholds, refine routing; deeper analytics; prepare final decision.
- Deliverables: Optimized prompts, improved SLAs, draft launch plan or extension rationale.
- Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Privacy/Legal
- Exit Criteria: CSAT targets achieved and FRT on track or better; ready for final decision.

Phase 6 – Final Decision & Closure (Week 12)
- Main Activities: Final measurements; decision meeting; create launch/revision/cancel plan; document lessons.
- Deliverables: Final report; decision memo; roadmap for next steps.
- Responsible Roles: PM, all, sponsor approval

5. Team Responsibilities
- Product Manager (PM): Roadmap ownership, risk management, cross-functional alignment, and final go/no-go facilitation.
- Machine Learning Engineer: Model prototyping, guardrails, prompt design, safety constraints, evaluation.
- Backend Engineer: Ticketing-system integration, data pipelines, logging, compliance controls, API wiring.
- Data Analyst (0.5 FTE): Metrics collection, baseline analysis, dashboards, post-pilot analytics.
- Customer Support Lead (0.5 FTE): Test coordination, domain knowledge, escalation policy validation.
- Privacy & Legal Specialist (0.25 FTE): Data handling reviews, privacy-by-design, compliance checks, risk mitigation.
- Testing Agents (8): Provide qualitative feedback and interaction data within 2 hours/week.

6. Budget Allocation
Budget Category | Amount | Purpose | Priority if Budget Is Reduced
- Personnel (PM, ML, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead, Privacy/Lex) | 95,000 | Core development, testing, governance, compliance
- Platform & Tools / Compute | 34,000 | AI hosting, monitoring, security tooling
- Testing & Pilot Operations | 18,000 | Agent participation, training, helpdesk time
- Privacy, Security, & Compliance | 12,000 | Legal reviews, data handling audits
- Contingency / Risk Reserve | 21,000 | Unexpected needs, scope tweaks
- Documentation and Change Management | 0 | Included in PM effort
Total: 180,000

7. Pilot Design
- Scope: Draft-and-route AI responses for non-financial interactions; escalate to humans for complex or disallowed actions; no automatic refunds or account changes.
- Data handling: No payment-card data in inputs; logs scrubbed; role-limited access; encryption in transit and at rest.
- Safety rails: High-risk categories trigger escalation; prompts include explicit instructions to escalate.
- Agent involvement: 8 agents capped at 2 hours/week; provide feedback on accuracy, tone, and escalation needs.
- Evaluation: Week 6 and Week 9 checkpoints; baseline FRT and CSAT; uphold privacy and safety controls; adjust scope if thresholds aren’t progressing.

8. Success Metrics and Decision Thresholds
Metric | Baseline | Target | Measurement Method | Go/No-Go Threshold
- Average First-Response Time (FRT) | 18 hours | ≤ 8 hours | System logs; ticket timestamps | Median FRT ≤ 8 hours for 2 consecutive weeks
- Customer Satisfaction (CSAT) | 78% | ≥ 84% | Post-interaction surveys | CSAT ≥ 84% in final week and sustained for 2 weeks
- Repetitive Workload Reduction | Baseline high | ≥ 25% reduction | Agent activity logs; ticket category analysis | Reduction ≥ 25% by Week 12
- Safety Violations | 0 | 0 | Incident log review | Zero unsafe actions; all escalations to humans
- Privacy Compliance | 0 | 0 | Privacy audits | No data leaks; compliant handling
- Budget Adherence | ≤ $180,000 | ≤ $180,000 | Financial tracking | Use within cap; apply disruption plan if cuts occur

9. Risk Register
Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Early Warning Sign | Mitigation | Contingency Owner
- Budget reduction at Week 6 | Medium | High | Spending pace nearing limit | Prioritize essential features; carve-out scope; pause non-critical work | PM
- Safety/Unauthorized action slipping | Medium | High | Elevated near-misses/escalations | Strengthen prompts; add filters; enhance human-in-the-loop | ML Engineer
- Data privacy breach | Low | Very High | Audit findings; access anomalies | Strict access controls; data masking; audits | Privacy/Legal
- Poor agent acceptance | Medium | Medium | Negative agent feedback | Engage agents early; refine prompts; coaching | Support Lead
- Insufficient CSAT improvement | Medium | High | Week 9 CSAT lag | Targeted improvements; potential extension | PM
- Integration outages | Low | Medium | Service degradation | Redundant tests; rollback plans; monitoring | Backend

10. Disruption-Response Plans
- Disruption A: End of Week 6 budget reduced by 20%
  - Response: Re-prioritize to essential guardrails and drafts; defer non-critical enhancements; re-baseline metrics; proceed if core KPIs achievable with reduced scope.
- Disruption B: End of Week 9 faster FRT but CSAT not reached
  - Response: Analyze drivers; tune prompts, tone, routing; targeted coaching; consider extension with documented rationale.

11. Final Decision Framework
- Launch: All primary metrics met (FRT ≤ 8 hours; CSAT ≥ 84%; sustained 2 weeks); zero safety/privacy issues; budget discipline.
- Limited Extension or Revision: If FRT met but CSAT lagging or safety near threshold; extend 2–4 weeks with tightened scope and milestones; document rationale and updated thresholds.
- Cancellation: Core goals unmet (FRT/CSAT below targets, safety/privacy issues, or budget unsustainable); discontinue with learnings and a revised non-deployment plan.

12. Conclusion
A tightly scoped, safety-forward pilot with human-in-the-loop safeguards enables evidence-based Week 12 go/no-go decisions. The plan emphasizes privacy, guardrails, and disciplined budgeting while targeting substantial gains in FRT and CSAT. With clearly defined roles, exit criteria, and disruption contingencies, the team can determine whether to launch, extend, or cancel based on objective, auditable results.

Note: All assumptions are explicit; no completed pilot results are claimed.

Phase 5: Pilot Expansion & Optimization (Weeks 9–11)
- Activities: Implement refinements; routing and thresholds; analytics; prepare decision.
- Deliverables: Optimized prompts; improved SLAs; decision rationale.
- Exit Criteria: CSAT and FRT on target trajectory; readiness for Week 12 decision.
Phase 6: Final Decision & Closure (Week 12)
- Activities: Final measurements; decision meeting; plan for launch/revision/cancel.
- Deliverables: Final report; decision memo; next steps roadmap.
- Exit Criteria: Go/No-Go decision documented.

Team Responsibilities (kept as stated, with emphasis on verification)
- PM: Roadmap, risk, Week 12-go/no-go facilitation.
- ML Engineer: Model prompts, guardrails, safety constraints.
- Backend Engineer: Ticketing integration, data pipelines, logging.
- Data Analyst: Baselines, dashboards, analytics.
- Support Lead: Test coordination, escalation policy validation.
- Privacy/Legal: Data handling, privacy validation, audits.
- 8 Testing Agents: Qualitative feedback; interaction data.

Budget Allocation (must be exact $180,000)
- Personnel: $95,000
- Platform & Compute: $34,000
- Testing & Pilot Ops: $18,000
- Privacy, Security, & Compliance: $12,000
- Contingency / Risk Reserve: $21,000
- Documentation: $0
Total: $180,000
Note: Before execution, confirm weekly burn rates align with Disruption A plans and that any re-scoping under budget cuts preserves the mandatory guardrails.

Pilot Design Essentials (reinforced)
- Scope: Draft/route AI responses; escalate for complex/disallowed actions; no autonomous refunds or account changes.
- Data: No payment-card data in inputs; logs scrubbed; encryption at rest/in transit.
- Safety: High-risk detections escalate to humans; explicit escalation prompts.
- Agents: 8 agents, max 2h/week.
- Evaluation: Week 6 and 9 checkpoints; preserve privacy/safety; adjust scope if thresholds aren’t progressing.

Success Metrics and Decision Thresholds (reiterated)
- FRT baseline 18h; target ≤ 8h by Week 12; med ≤ 8h for 2 consecutive weeks.
- CSAT baseline 78%; target ≥ 84% by Week 12; sustained 2 weeks.
- Repetitive Workload: ≥25% reduction by Week 12.
- Safety: 0 violations.
- Privacy: 0 violations.
- Budget: ≤ $180,000; disruption plans invoked if reduced.

Disruption Plans (robust)
- Disruption A (Week 6 -20% budget): Re-prioritize to essential guardrails and drafts; defer non-critical enhancements; re-baseline metrics; proceed if core KPIs achievable with reduced scope.
- Disruption B (Week 9 - faster FRT but CSAT lag): Diagnose drivers; tune prompts and routing; consider extension with rationale; decide extension or revised thresholds.

Final Decision Framework (Week 12)
- Launch: All primary metrics met; safe/privacy posture; budget kept; ready for full deployment.
- Limited Extension/Revision: If FRT OK but CSAT lag or safety gate near threshold; extend 2–4 weeks with tightened scope; document rationale.
- Cancellation: Core goals unmet or safety/privacy breached; discontinue with documented learnings.

Conclusion
The plan is structurally solid for a data-driven, privacy-forward pilot with human-in-the-loop safeguards. However, to satisfy Critic’s mandate, address the explicit Week-8 decision log, tighten data-flow documentation, and finalize measurement schemas for the repetitive-work reduction and CSAT drivers. Ensure all data handling, guardrails, and budget contingencies are auditable and sign-off ready before Week 7.

Verification and Next Steps (Critic role)
- Produce Week-8 Decision Log draft with required sign-offs.
- Deliver Data Handling Appendix and Data Map with masking rules.
- Deliver KPI measurement schema for FRT, CSAT, and repetitive-work reduction.
- Confirm PCI/PII exclusion in model inputs and logs.
- Schedule a pre-Week-8 governance review to secure go/no-go alignment.

[Adaptive gap review]
Executive summary of Critic assessment
- The plan is coherent, with explicit constraints, a clear go/no-go framework, and a budget totaling $180,000. It foregrounds safety, privacy, and human-in-the-loop controls while aiming to achieve FRT ≤8 hours and CSAT ≥84%.
- Key gaps to close before execution:
  1) Week-8 Decision Log: require an explicit, sign-offable go/no-go criteria (documented thresholds, approvals, and contingency actions).
  2) Data flow and masking: a complete Data Handling Appendix mapping every input source, what is scrubbed/masked, and exact access controls; confirm PCI/PII exclusion with verifiable controls.
  3) Repetitive-work reduction definition: specify taxonomy and measurement method (e.g., non-value-added interactions per 1,000 tickets, or a clearly defined category-set reduction) and ensure instrumentation captures it.
  4) Minimum viable feature set for disruption A: codify exactly which guardrails and drafting capabilities remain non-negotiable under a 20% budget reduction, plus revised KPI targets.
  5) Week-12 decision criteria: ensure a precise threshold for “Launch,” “Limited Extension,” and “Cancellation” with sign-offs and an auditable post-pilot deployment plan.
  6) Baseline refreshes: confirm CSAT and FRT baselines reflect the current month data to avoid optimistic targets.
  7) Safety assurance memo: require a formal Safety Assurance Report covering guardrails coverage and Phase 2–3 test results.

Gaps and proposed corrections (critical items)
- Week 8 Decision Log: Create a standalone document template with:
  - Current KPIs (FRT, CSAT, workload reduction), target trajectories, and durations (must reach median FRT ≤8h for 2 consecutive weeks; CSAT ≥84% sustained two weeks).
  - Approvals: PM, Privacy/Legal, and relevant Finance/CTO sign-offs.
  - Budget status: burn-rate against plan; cut-in features list if budget reduced.
  - Contingencies: explicit go/no-go criteria if targets are not met, including extension or cancellation pathways.
- Data Flow Appendix:
  - Data sources: ticket content, metadata, agent-only annotations; explicitly list inputs fed to the model.
  - Data masking: redaction rules for PII, non-essential fields, and any PII-like tokens; demonstrate masking in training/inference logs.
  - PCI/PII controls: confirm no PCI data present; provide access-control matrix by role; audit-ready data-flow diagrams.
- Repetitive Workload Metric:
  - Define categories of repetitive questions (e.g., order status, shipping times, return policy) with a baseline count.
  - Specify measurement method (e.g., count of replies in the same category per 1000 tickets that are AI-drafted vs agent-handled) and cadence.
  - Ensure telemetry captures category-level distributions for pre/post comparison.
- Minimum Viable Feature Set for Disruption A:
  - List features retained under a 20% budget cut (e.g., drafting capability, routing, escalation rules, safety rails) and which are deprioritized (e.g., advanced analytics dashboards, secondary language support, extensive SLAs).
  - Tie each retained feature to revised KPIs and an updated Week-8 decision framework.
- Week-12 Decision Thresholds:
  - Launch: all primary metrics met (median FRT ≤8h for 2 consecutive weeks; CSAT ≥84% sustained for 2 weeks; zero safety violations; within budget).
  - Limited Extension: FRT achieved; CSAT lagging or safety margin narrow; extend 2–4 weeks with re-scoped milestones and explicit rationale.
  - Cancellation: FRT and/or CSAT not on track, safety/privacy issues persist, or budget unsustainable; discontinue with documented learnings.
- Baseline Revalidation:
  - Recompute FRT/CSAT baselines using latest month data to avoid drift; document methodology and sampling.
- Safety Assurance:
  - Provide a formal Safety Assurance Report detailing guardrails coverage, escalation paths, and test results from Phases 2–3.

Compact risk, disruption, and decision framework notes
- Week-6 budget cut disruption: prioritize essential guardrails and drafting capabilities; deprioritize non-critical analytics; adjust Week-8 criteria accordingly; ensure core KPIs achievable with the reduced scope.
- Week-9 CSAT shortfall despite faster FRT: diagnose drivers (tone, routing, escalation latency); apply targeted prompt/tone/categorization refinements; consider a measured extension with documented rationale.

Minimal useful reallocation (actionable)
- Reallocate 10–15% of Platform & Tools budget to Privacy & Compliance and Safety Assurance memo production, to strengthen data handling and risk documentation.
- If required by disruption A, shift Contingency Reserve funding to cover critical guardrails and escalation tooling upgrades, preserving the MVP’s safety envelope.
- Freeze non-critical KPI instrumentation enhancements until Week-8 decision, but ensure baseline and essential metrics remain captured.

Required outputs (as Critic)
- Week-8 Decision Log draft: include performance status, approvals, updated targets, contingency actions, and sign-offs.
- Data Handling Appendix: data map, masking rules, access-control matrix, PCI/PII exclusion validation.
- KPI measurement schema for FRT, CSAT, and Repetitive-Work Reduction, with evidence collection methods.
- Explicit MVP feature-set for Disruption A, with remaining guardrails and their non-negotiable status.
- Final Week-12 decision memo template and post-pilot deployment plan.
- Baseline refresh documentation: confirm latest FRT and CSAT baselines used in assessment.

Conclusion
The SP-04 plan is robust and budget-aligned, with clear Go/No-Go gates. To satisfy the Critic role, promptly supply explicit Week-8 governance artifacts, a complete data-flow appendix, and precise KPI definitions and baselines. These refinements will convert the plan into an auditable, execution-ready blueprint, enabling a defensible Week-12 decision between Launch, Limited Extension, or Cancellation.

Instruction:
Produce a self-contained final deliverable from the adapted work. Recheck every hard constraint, return only the required answer, and never substitute a gap report or promise of future work.

Return only the content for your role. Do not include hidden chain-of-thought. Use concise reasoning summaries where useful.

final_output:
1. Executive Summary
This 12-week pilot assesses an AI-powered customer-support assistant for a mid-sized e-commerce company (~50k requests/month). The pilot drafts responses and routes to human agents, with strict guardrails: no autonomous refunds, account changes, or payment disputes; payment-card data is excluded from inputs. A fixed budget of $180,000 funds a lean team and testing, culminating in a Week 12 go/no-go decision to launch, extend/revise, or cancel. Two disruption scenarios are covered: (A) Week 6 budget cut of 20%, and (B) Week 9 faster FRT but CSAT shortfall. The plan emphasizes safety, privacy, measurable FRT/CSAT improvements, and a rigorous decision framework enabled by clearly assigned roles, milestones, and exit criteria.

2. Strategic Objectives and Priorities
- Primary objective: Demonstrate AI-assisted workflow achieving FRT ≤ 8 hours and CSAT ≥ 84%, with ≥25% reduction in repetitive workload.
- Safeguards: No automated refunds/account changes/payments; high-risk actions escalated; privacy-by-design; no payment-card data in inputs.
- Operational discipline: MVP with gated drafts, routing, escalation rails, and dashboards; weekly governance.
- Decision gates: Week 8 limited-pilot go/no-go; Week 12 final decision to launch, extend/revise, or cancel.

3. Assumptions and Scope Boundaries
- Assumptions:
  - Eight agents participate up to 2 hours/week each.
  - Pilot integrates with existing helpdesk/ticketing; no standalone portal.
  - Payment disputes remain human-only; PCI/PII data not included in model inputs.
  - Core team availability for 12 weeks: PM, ML Engineer, Backend Engineer, 0.5 FTE Data Analyst, 0.5 FTE Support Lead, 0.25 FTE Privacy/Legal.
- Scope Boundaries:
  - In-scope: AI-generated drafts, recommended actions, routing/escalation logic, and reporting dashboards.
  - Out-of-scope: Automated refunds, account changes, payment-dispute resolutions; full production deployment without go-ahead.
  - Budget: Exactly $180,000; disruption handling per plan.

4. Twelve-Week Phased Roadmap
Phase 1: Discovery & Design (Weeks 1–2)
- Main Activities: Requirements refinement, safety rails, data handling plan, privacy/legal alignment, baseline metrics.
- Deliverables: Data-handling appendix, guardrail specifications, data flows, baseline metrics.
- Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Privacy/Legal
- Exit Criteria: Sign-off on data constraints, escalation rules, MLOps plan; baseline metrics captured.

Phase 2: MVP Build & Guardrails (Weeks 3–4)
- Main Activities: Draft-response generator, routing, escalation triggers, safety guardrails, mock data tests.
- Deliverables: MVP with escalation rules, test suite, compliance checklist.
- Responsible Roles: ML Engineer, Backend, PM, Privacy/Legal, Support Lead
- Exit Criteria: Internal safety pass; ready for controlled testing.

Phase 3: Internal Testing & Refinement (Weeks 5–6)
- Main Activities: 8 agents testing up to 2 hours each; refine prompts/guardrails; monitor privacy/logs; budget tracking.
- Deliverables: Test results, guardrail adjustments, updated prompts, usage dashboards.
- Responsible Roles: ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead, PM
- Exit Criteria: Positive drafts; no unsafe actions; budget within plan; ready for limited pilot (Week 7 start if budget allows).

Phase 4: Limited Pilot Launch (Weeks 7–8)
- Main Activities: Live pilot with real tickets; real-time monitoring; feedback loops; weekly governance.
- Deliverables: Live pilot environment, weekly performance updates, escalation protocol in action.
- Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead
- Exit Criteria: Week 8 review; go/no-go decision aligned to budget and performance thresholds.

Phase 5: Pilot Expansion & Optimization (Weeks 9–11)
- Main Activities: Implement improvements; adjust thresholds; deeper analytics; prepare final decision.
- Deliverables: Optimized prompts; improved SLAs; decision rationale.
- Responsible Roles: PM, ML Engineer, Backend, Data Analyst, Privacy/Legal
- Exit Criteria: CSAT targets on track; FRT on track or better; ready for final decision.

Phase 6: Final Decision & Closure (Week 12)
- Main Activities: Final measurements; decision meeting; plan for launch/revision/cancel; lessons.
- Deliverables: Final report; decision memo; roadmap for next steps.
- Responsible Roles: PM, all

5. Team Responsibilities
- Product Manager (PM): Roadmap ownership, risk management, cross-functional alignment, Week 12 decision facilitation.
- Machine Learning Engineer: Model prototyping, guardrails, prompt design, safety constraints, evaluation.
- Backend Engineer: Ticketing integration, data pipelines, logging, compliance controls.
- Data Analyst (0.5 FTE): Metrics collection, baseline analysis, dashboards, post-pilot analytics.
- Customer Support Lead (0.5 FTE): Test coordination, domain knowledge, escalation policy validation.
- Privacy & Legal Specialist (0.25 FTE): Data handling reviews, privacy-by-design, compliance checks.
- Testing Agents (8): Qualitative feedback and interaction data within 2 hours/week.

6. Budget Allocation
Budget Category | Amount | Purpose | Priority if Budget Is Reduced
- Personnel (PM, ML, Backend, Data Analyst, Support Lead, Privacy/Lex) | 95,000 | Core development, testing, governance, compliance
- Platform & Tools / Compute | 34,000 | AI hosting, monitoring, security tooling
- Testing & Pilot Operations | 18,000 | Agent participation, training, helpdesk time
- Privacy, Security, & Compliance | 12,000 | Legal reviews, data handling audits
- Contingency / Risk Reserve | 21,000 | Unexpected needs, scope tweaks
- Documentation & Change Management | 0 | Included in PM effort
Total: 180,000

7. Pilot Design
- Scope: Draft-and-route AI responses for non-financial interactions; escalate to humans for complex or disallowed actions; no automatic refunds or account changes.
- Data handling: No payment-card data in inputs; logs scrubbed; role-limited access; encryption in transit and at rest.
- Safety rails: High-risk categories trigger escalation; prompts include explicit escalation instructions.
- Agent involvement: 8 agents capped at 2 hours/week; provide feedback on accuracy, tone, escalation needs.
- Evaluation: Week 6 and Week 9 checkpoints; baseline FRT and CSAT; privacy/safety controls upheld; adjust scope if thresholds aren’t progressing.

8. Success Metrics and Decision Thresholds
Metric | Baseline | Target | Measurement Method | Go/No-Go Threshold
- Average First-Response Time (FRT) | 18 hours | ≤ 8 hours | System logs; ticket timestamps | Median ≤ 8 hours for 2 consecutive weeks
- Customer Satisfaction (CSAT) | 78% | ≥ 84% | Post-interaction surveys | CSAT ≥ 84% in final week and sustained for 2 weeks
- Repetitive Workload Reduction | High baseline | ≥ 25% reduction | Agent activity logs; ticket-category analysis | Reduction ≥ 25% by Week 12
- Safety Violations | 0 | 0 | Incident log review | Zero unsafe actions; all escalations to humans
- Privacy Compliance | 0 | 0 | Privacy audits | No data leaks; compliant handling
- Budget Adherence | ≤ $180,000 | ≤ $180,000 | Financial tracking | Use within cap; apply disruption plan if cuts occur

9. Risk Register
Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Early Warning Sign | Mitigation | Contingency Owner
- Budget reduction at Week 6 | Medium | High | Spending pace nearing limit | Prioritize essential features; carve-out scope; pause non-critical work | PM
- Safety/Unauthorized action slipping | Medium | High | Increased near-misses/escalations | Strengthen prompts; add filters; enhanced human-in-the-loop | ML Engineer
- Data privacy breach | Low | Very High | Audit findings; access anomalies | Strict access controls; data masking; audits | Privacy/Legal
- Poor agent acceptance | Medium | Medium | Negative agent feedback | Engage agents early; refine prompts; coaching | Support Lead
- Insufficient CSAT improvement | Medium | High | Week 9 CSAT lag | Targeted improvements; potential extension | PM
- Integration outages | Low | Medium | Service degradation | Redundant tests; rollback plans; monitoring | Backend

10. Disruption-Response Plans
- Disruption A: End of Week 6 budget reduced by 20%
  - Action: Re-prioritize to essential guardrails and drafts; defer non-critical enhancements; re-baseline metrics.
  - Go/No-Go criterion: If core KPIs are achievable with reduced scope, proceed to limited pilot; otherwise adjust Week-8 criteria.
  - Owner: PM (with Privacy/Legal sign-off)
- Disruption B: End of Week 9 faster FRT but CSAT not reached
  - Action: Diagnose dissatisfaction drivers; tune prompts, tone, routing; targeted coaching; consider extension with documented rationale.
  - Go/No-Go criterion: If CSAT remains below target, extension only with documented rationale; else proceed to final decision.

11. Final Decision Framework
- Launch: All primary metrics met (FRT ≤ 8 hours; CSAT ≥ 84%; sustained 2 weeks); zero safety/privacy issues; budget within $180k. Proceed to full deployment planning.
- Limited Extension or Revision: FRT achieved but CSAT lagging or safety near threshold; extend 2–4 weeks with tightened scope and milestones; document rationale and updated targets.
- Cancellation: Core goals unmet (FRT/CSAT not improving; safety/privacy issues; budget unsustainable); discontinue pilot with learnings and a revised non-deployment plan.

12. Conclusion
The plan provides a disciplined, privacy-forward pilot with strong safety guardrails and human-in-the-loop controls, designed to yield a defensible Week 12 decision. To satisfy execution-readiness, produce Week-8 governance artifacts, a complete Data Handling Appendix, and precise KPI baselines and measurement schemas. Ensure auditable data flows, guardrails, and budget contingencies are sign-off-ready before Week 7.

Notes on critical preparations (Gaps to close before Stage Gate)
- Week-8 Decision Log draft with sign-offs from PM, Privacy/Legal, and finance.
- Data Handling Appendix detailing inputs, masking rules, access controls, and PCI/PII exclusions.
- KPI measurement schema for FRT, CSAT, and Repetitive-Work Reduction with data collection methods.
- MVP feature-set for Disruption A under budget reduction, with non-negotiable guardrails preserved.
- Week-12 decision memo template and post-pilot deployment plan.

agent_task.prompt:
Create a 12-week strategy for piloting and deciding whether to launch an AI-powered customer-support assistant for a mid-sized e-commerce company.

The company currently handles approximately 50,000 customer-support requests per month.

Current performance:
- Average first-response time: 18 hours
- Customer satisfaction score: 78%
- Support agents frequently spend time answering repetitive questions
- Refunds, account changes, and payment disputes require human approval

The company wants the pilot to achieve the following goals:
- Reduce average first-response time to 8 hours or less
- Increase customer satisfaction to at least 84%
- Reduce repetitive workload for support agents
- Avoid unsafe or unauthorized actions
- Protect customer and payment-related information

The project has the following constraints:
- Total budget: $180,000
- Total duration: 12 weeks
- A limited pilot must begin by the end of Week 8
- A final launch, revision, or cancellation decision must be made by the end of Week 12
- The system may draft responses and recommend actions, but it may not independently issue refunds, modify accounts, or resolve payment disputes
- Payment-card information must not be included in model inputs
- Project team: 1 Product Manager, 1 Machine Learning Engineer, 1 Backend Engineer, 0.5 Full-Time Data Analyst, 0.5 Full-Time Customer Support Lead, and 0.25 Full-Time Privacy and Legal Specialist
- Eight customer-support agents may participate in testing for no more than two hours per person per week

Develop a practical strategy that balances delivery speed, service quality, employee workload, privacy, safety, and budget.

The plan must also address two possible disruptions:
1. At the end of Week 6, the available budget may be reduced by 20%.
2. At the end of Week 9, the pilot may show faster response times but fail to reach the customer-satisfaction target.

The response must include: an executive summary; strategic priorities; assumptions and scope boundaries; a complete 12-week roadmap; team responsibilities; a budget totaling exactly $180,000; pilot design; success metrics and go/no-go thresholds; a risk register with at least six risks; disruption-response plans; and a final decision framework distinguishing launch, limited extension or revision, and cancellation.

Use the following required tables:
1. Roadmap: Phase and Weeks | Main Activities | Deliverables | Responsible Roles | Exit Criteria
2. Budget: Budget Category | Amount | Purpose | Priority if Budget Is Reduced
3. Success Metrics: Metric | Baseline | Target | Measurement Method | Go/No-Go Threshold
4. Risk Register: Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Early Warning Sign | Mitigation | Contingency Owner

The response should be approximately 1,000-1,400 words. Use only the information and constraints provided. Clearly label any additional assumptions. Do not invent completed pilot results or claim that the strategy has already been tested.
